cards.back: A counting technique used to compute the number of elements in the union of multiple sets by considering their intersections.
A variation of the principle that accounts for the intersections of intersections of sets, and is used to solve problems involving more complex sets.
A permutation of the elements of a set, such that no element is in its original position.
A polynomial that counts the number of derangements of a set of n elements, and is named after the rook, a piece in chess that can move diagonally.
The number of derangements of a set of n elements is given by the formula !n = n! * (1/0! - 1/1! + 1/2! - ... + (-1)^n/n!) where !n is the number of derangements of n elements.
